overview.body: Module 5 begins with a question: what if a city knew things about itself? Right now, city data lives in a dictionary. The city name is a key. Its connections are a nested dict. Its trivia is another dict. The functions that work on that data are scattered across your file. A class changes that. A City object holds its own name, type, tagline, connections, and trivia. The methods that work on that data live right next to it. This week you define the City class and write a test program that puts it through its paces. Nothing about the game changes yet. You are just building a better container for what already exists.

mentorQuote.quote: A dictionary can hold city data. A class holds city data AND knows what to do with it. That is the difference between a filing cabinet and a coworker. Both have the information. Only one can act on it.

objectives: Define a class using the class keyword with an __init__ constructor
Store data in private attributes using the double-underscore naming convention
Write getter methods to safely expose private attribute values
Write functional methods that operate on the object's own data
Create City objects and call methods using dot notation
Implement the __str__ special method for clean printed output
overview.keyConceptsText: Chapter 10: Program 10-12 (cellphone.py, class definition, private attributes, getters and setters) and Program 10-9 (bankaccount2.py, __str__ method and methods with logic).
overview.textbookReference: Starting Out with Python, 6th Edition by Tony Gaddis, Chapter 10
programRequirements: city.py defines the City class with __init__, private attributes, getter methods, connection methods, trivia methods, status methods, and __str__
city_test.py imports City, creates at least 4 City objects, adds connections and trivia, and tests all major methods
Both files have top-of-file documentation headers: Name, abc123, Section, Description
overview.additionalFilesNote: Submit both city.py (the class) and city_test.py (the test program). CodeGrade imports city.py directly and tests the class methods in isolation.
overview.filenameNote: Submit city.py and city_test.py this week.
overview.saveYourCode: Keep city.py. Module 5.2 adds the Player class alongside it, and 5.3 extends it with WinCity and DeadEndCity subclasses.

rows.task: Define City class, __init__, and private attributes
Add getter methods and __str__ for clean printing
Create objects, add data, test every method, verify output

beforeYouBegin.intro: Here is how your finished program fits together. This week you are building two new files from scratch. Nothing carries forward from Module 4 directly. The City class is the foundation everything else in Module 5 builds on.
beforeYouBegin.architecture:
# ---- city.py ----
class City:
│
│── __init__(name, city_type, tagline)    [NEW] sets up private attrs
│   self.__name          string
│   self.__city_type     'START','HUB','WIN','DEAD_END','WRONG_WAY'
│   self.__tagline       string
│   self.__connections   {}   dict: city_name -> distance
│   self.__trivia        []   list of {question, answer} dicts
│
│── GETTERS
│   get_name()           returns self.__name
│   get_type()           returns self.__city_type
│   get_tagline()        returns self.__tagline
│   get_connection_count() returns len of connections dict
│   get_trivia_count()   returns len of trivia list
│
│── CONNECTION METHODS
│   add_connection(city_name, distance)  adds to __connections
│   get_distance_to(city_name)          dict .get(), returns -1 if missing
│   get_destination_names()             list(__connections.keys())
│
│── TRIVIA METHODS
│   add_trivia(question, answer)        appends to __trivia list
│   get_random_trivia()                 random.choice, returns None if empty
│
│── STATUS METHODS
│   is_win_city()        returns city_type == 'WIN'
│   is_dead_end()        returns city_type in ['DEAD_END','WRONG_WAY']
│
│── DISPLAY
│   display_info()       prints name, type, tagline, counts
│   display_destinations() prints numbered connection list
│   __str__()            returns 'Name (TYPE)'

# ---- city_test.py ----
from city import City

main():
│── Create 4 City objects (START, HUB, WIN, DEAD_END)
│── Test __str__ on each
│── Add connections, test get_distance_to()
│── Add trivia, test get_random_trivia()
│── Test display_info() and display_destinations()
│── Test is_win_city() and is_dead_end()

content.body: Create a new file called city.py. This file will contain only the City class definition, with no main() function and no if __name__ guard. The class is a module; the test program you write in CP5 is what runs it.

items: Add your top-of-file documentation header: Name, abc123, Section, and a one-sentence description of what city.py contains.
Define the class: class City:. Everything else in this file is indented inside this class.
Write the class docstring immediately after the class line: a one-line description of what a City object represents.

Write the __init__ method. It takes self plus three parameters: name, city_type, and tagline. Write a docstring that documents all three parameters. Store all three as private attributes using the double-underscore prefix: self.__name, self.__city_type, self.__tagline.

In the same __init__, initialize two more private attributes: self.__connections = {} (an empty dict for city connections) and self.__trivia = [] (an empty list for trivia questions). These are not parameters; they always start empty.

references.description: Shows exactly this pattern: class definition, __init__ constructor, and three private attributes stored with double-underscore prefix. The City class follows the same structure.

rows: Using single underscore _name instead of double __name
Attribute is still accessible from outside; encapsulation is not enforced
Use self.__name (two underscores) for private attributes
Forgetting self as the first parameter in __init__
Every method in a class must have self as its first parameter
IndentationError or __init__ becomes a standalone function
Every method must be indented one level inside the class

content.title: CP1 Complete
items: city.py exists with a top-of-file documentation header
class City: is defined with a class docstring
__init__(self, name, city_type, tagline) is defined with a docstring
Five private attributes initialized: __name, __city_type, __tagline, __connections, __trivia
File imports nothing yet and has no main(). That is correct

content.body: Private attributes cannot be read from outside the class directly. Getter methods are the controlled access points. Add the getters now, then add __str__ so that printing a City object gives clean readable output.

items: Add a comment # --- GETTER METHODS --- after the __init__ to organize the class.
Write get_name(self): docstring, return self.__name.
Write get_type(self): docstring, return self.__city_type.
Write get_tagline(self): docstring, return self.__tagline.
Write get_connection_count(self): docstring, return len(self.__connections).
Write get_trivia_count(self): docstring, return len(self.__trivia).
Write the __str__(self) method: return an f-string in the format 'CityName (CITY_TYPE)'. For example, a San Antonio object should print as 'San Antonio (START)'.

content.body: The __str__ method is what Python calls when you use print(city_object) or embed the object in an f-string with f'{city_object}'. Without it, printing a City gives you something like <city.City object at 0x7f4a...>. With it, you get San Antonio (START). It costs one method and pays back every time you debug or display an object.

content.title: CP2 Complete
items: Five getter methods added, each with a docstring
__str__ returns 'CityName (CITY_TYPE)' format
Quick test: open a Python shell, import City, create one object, and print it. Verify the output matches the format

content.body: The __connections dictionary maps destination city names to distances. These three methods let outside code add connections and query them without touching the dictionary directly.

items: Add a comment # --- CONNECTION METHODS --- to organize this section.
Write add_connection(self, city_name, distance): docstring describing both parameters. Body: self.__connections[city_name] = distance.
Write get_distance_to(self, city_name): docstring noting it returns -1 if the city is not connected. Body: use self.__connections.get(city_name, -1).
Write get_destination_names(self): docstring. Body: return list(self.__connections.keys()).
Write display_destinations(self): print 'From [city_name], you can travel to:', then loop through self.__connections.items() with a numbered counter and print each destination and distance.

references.description: The __connections dictionary follows the same key-value pattern as the card deck. Adding a connection is the same as adding a card. get_distance_to() uses the same .get(key, default) pattern.

content.title: CP3 Complete
items: add_connection(), get_distance_to(), get_destination_names(), and display_destinations() all defined with docstrings
get_distance_to() returns -1 for a city that is not connected
display_destinations() prints a numbered list

checkpoints.title: Add Trivia, Status, and Display Methods

content.body: Four more methods finish the class: trivia storage and retrieval, win/dead-end status checks, and a full city info display.

items: Add # --- TRIVIA METHODS ---. Write add_trivia(self, question, answer): append a dictionary {'question': question, 'answer': answer} to self.__trivia.
Write get_random_trivia(self): add import random at the top of the method (or at the top of the file). If self.__trivia is empty, return None. Otherwise return random.choice(self.__trivia).
Add # --- STATUS METHODS ---. Write is_win_city(self): return self.__city_type == 'WIN'.
Write is_dead_end(self): return True if self.__city_type is 'DEAD_END' or 'WRONG_WAY'.
Write display_info(self): print the city name, type, tagline, connection count, and trivia count. Use the getter methods rather than accessing the private attributes directly.

content.title: Use Your Own Getters Inside the Class
content.body: Inside display_info(), call self.get_connection_count() rather than len(self.__connections) directly. Using your own methods internally is good practice. If the implementation of connection storage ever changes, only get_connection_count() needs updating.

content.title: CP4 Complete
items: add_trivia() appends a dict with 'question' and 'answer' keys
get_random_trivia() returns None for empty list, random dict otherwise
is_win_city() and is_dead_end() return the correct boolean for each city type
display_info() prints all five city attributes using getter methods

content.body: Create a second file called city_test.py. This is your test harness. It imports the City class and methodically exercises every method you just wrote. A class is only as good as its tests.

items: Add your documentation header, then from city import City.
Write a main() function. Start with a header line: print('=' * 50), print('CITY CLASS TEST'), print('=' * 50).

Create four City objects: San Antonio ('START', 'Home of the Alamo'), Houston ('GATEWAY', 'Space City'), Corpus Christi ('WIN', 'Sparkling City by the Sea'), and Laredo ('DEAD_END', 'Gateway to Mexico').
Test __str__: print all four objects using f-strings and verify the output format.

Add connections: San Antonio to Houston (200), Corpus Christi (150), and Laredo (160). Houston to San Antonio (200). Test get_distance_to() for a connected city and for a city that is not connected. Verify you get -1 for the missing one.
Add two trivia questions to San Antonio. Call get_random_trivia() and print the question and answer.
Call display_info() and display_destinations() on San Antonio.
Test is_win_city() and is_dead_end() on all four cities and print the results.
Add if __name__ == '__main__': main() at the bottom. Run the file and verify every section produces the expected output.

content.quote: I used to skip writing test programs and just submit. Then I started failing CodeGrade tests for methods I was sure worked. Now I test every method manually before I submit anything. It takes ten minutes and saves an hour.

content.output:
==================================================
CITY CLASS TEST
==================================================

Testing __str__:
  San Antonio (START)
  Houston (GATEWAY)
  Corpus Christi (WIN)
  Laredo (DEAD_END)

Testing distances:
  SA to Houston: 200 miles
  SA to Dallas: -1 miles

Testing display_info:
City: San Antonio
Type: START
Tagline: "Home of the Alamo"
Connections: 3
Trivia Questions: 2

Testing display_destinations:
From San Antonio, you can travel to:
  1. Houston - 200 miles
  2. Corpus Christi - 150 miles
  3. Laredo - 160 miles

Testing status methods:
  Corpus is win city: True
  Laredo is dead end: True
  Houston is win city: False

All tests complete!

content.title: Lab 5.1 Complete
items: city.py defines the City class with all methods and docstrings
city_test.py imports City, creates 4 objects, and tests every method
Output matches the expected output block above
Both files have documentation headers
Both files submitted to CodeGrade

beforeYouSubmit: city.py contains the City class only, with no main() and no if __name__ guard
All five private attributes initialized in __init__: __name, __city_type, __tagline, __connections, __trivia
All getter methods present: get_name(), get_type(), get_tagline(), get_connection_count(), get_trivia_count()
Connection methods present: add_connection(), get_distance_to(), get_destination_names(), display_destinations()
city_test.py tests every method and matches expected output
Top-of-file documentation headers in both files
finalChecklist.scopeReminder: Chapter 10 techniques: class definition, __init__ constructor, private attributes with double underscore, getter methods, __str__ special method, dot notation method calls, and creating objects. All Module 1–4 techniques also permitted.

permittedTechniques.notPermitted: list comprehensions, inheritance (Module 5.3 topic), multiple inheritance

warnings: CodeGrade imports city.py directly and tests each method in isolation. A syntax error in city.py will fail all tests.

ifYouGetStuck: Re-read Program 10-12 (cellphone.py) in your BookEx. It is the same pattern as the City class with different data
If you get AttributeError when calling a method, check that self is the first parameter in that method's definition
If print(city_object) shows a memory address instead of a name, __str__ is either missing or has a typo in the method name
Submit to CodeGrade early. It tells you exactly which methods pass and which do not
needHelp.bookexResourceNote: Chapter 10 BookEx Reference Guide, which covers Programs 10-12 and 10-9 showing the class definition pattern this lab uses
